sentences.text: My full name?
Liselotte Ruth Steckl.
Liselotte Sander.
L-I-S-E-L-O-T-T-E [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] S-A-N-D-E-R.
Jihlava, or Iglau in German, in Czechoslovakia.
J-I-H-L-A-V-A and Iglau is spelled I-G-L-A-U.
Franz Sander and Margarete Buchsbaum.
That's her maiden name.
Both of them?
F-R-A-N-Z S-A-N-D-E-R and Margarete is spelled M-A-R-G-A-R-E-T-E and Buchsbaum is B-U-C-H-S-B-A-U-M.
I had one brother.
Hans, or Hanus in Czech.
Well, my parents owned a small manufacturing company.
They were making paper bags.
We had about nine employees or 10 employees.
I don't remember exactly.
We were a middle class family, doing quite well.
I had a very good childhood.
My home life was definitely satisfactory.
My parents were strict but very loving.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think we were spoiled, but we certainly got most of the stuff that we wanted.
We did quite a bit of traveling.
I did a lot of sports.
I played tennis, I skied, I skated.
I played table tennis.
Actually, I was quite good at it.
I played for the school.
And that's about all I can remember.
Obviously, I went to school.
And I was a reasonably good student.
Actually, very good.
I went to a German school.
The Jews in my hometown since Iglau really originally was a German town, they're mostly German speaking, even after World War I, when Czechoslovakia became independent.
And I think that's one of the things that the Czechs really didn't like, that all the Jews spoke German.

And I was sent to a German school, whereas my brother, who is four years younger, already went to a Czech school, because of the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how should I say-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] resentment of the Czechs.
Jihlava in my time was about 50-50.
But it was still considered the German island.
All the surrounding villages were German.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] most of, as I said, most of the Jews spoke German.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to first-- I had my five years of elementary school, which I remember as quite pleasant.
I was a very good student.
I had nothing but ones, except in singing, I had a two, and that was very bad.
And then I went to the gymnasium.
That's the secondary school, which has eight grades and is considered in preparation for universities.
And after sixth grade, which was 1938, I decided to quit the German school and transfer to a Czech school.
You asked about the relationship, however.
And I had a number of German friends, and we were-- you know, they were all very friendly.
I did not have any problems, prior to 1938.
We were, in my class, we were 30.
We were divided into boys and girls.
And out of the 30 girls that I went to the secondary school with, there were five Jewish girls.
And I was friendly only really with one of the Jewish girls, and the rest of my friends were German girls.
Medium.
When I was a child, I remember it was a little more religious than later.
My grandmother, my mother's mother, lived with us.
And she used to be fairly religious.
My mother was medium religious, and my father was absolutely not religious.
So remembering how it worked in our house, I remember when I was a small child, I-- my mother lit candles on Shabbat or Friday evening.
And-- but later on, I don't remember that she ever lit candles.
I remember that as a smaller child, I wasn't allowed to do any work on Shabbat.
But I was allowed to write in school-- we went to school on Shabbat.
But at home wasn't allowed to sew, I wasn't allowed to write.
But as time went on, this also ceased.
And I wasn't allowed to go swimming on Shabbat.
But once I met my father in the street carrying his swimming trunks, so that ceased too.
So we went to temple on the high holidays mainly.
I think-- I don't think we went-- not only on high holidays.
We also went on the like, Pesach, and so on, but that was about it.
There was not too much religion going on.
We had a kosher household though.
But, [LAUGHS] it's really funny.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was also sort of lax.
My-- since my mother felt that the children should have certain things because they are good for them, so we had ham, for instance.
And the ham, in order for the fleishig plate not to get treif, so it was eaten on milchig plate.
[LAUGHS] I still remember that, it's so funny.
Or when we had salami, which we had sometimes, so we couldn't put it on butter.
We had to have it on a piece of paper, and the bread and butter on the plate.
So it had to be eaten sort of like this.
[LAUGHS] Everybody laughs about it when I recall those things.
Well, my father-- my father traveled quite a bit.
He-- we also had wholesale paper.
And so he was selling in the neighborhood villages.
And my grandmother worked in the store.
And my mother worked sort of part time.
She did some bookkeeping.
And part time, I don't know what she did.
She didn't cook a lot.
We had a live-in maid, part-time cook.
And actually, we had a nanny for my brother and I.
I came up with that better than governess.
[LAUGHS] And, you know, it's-- I don't know what it was like.
You went to school, and after school you eat a plate with somebody or went to somebody's house, or you came home did your homework.
My parents, like most Jewish parents, were very study-minded, so I had to do my homework.
I remember once waking up at 11 o'clock at night, having forgotten to my homework.
[LAUGHS] And I woke up-- my grand-- I slept with my grandmother in one room.
And so I woke up my grandmother, and she let me do my homework at 11 o'clock at night.
Actually, if I remember correctly, the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] firm belonged originally to my grandfather.
And my father sort of married into the firm.
So I think nominally my grandmother was still owner, I'm not 100% sure, but somehow I remember that.
And my father, I, I really don't know what he was called-- what his title was.
But he was obviously running the place.
Well, my father was a big Zionist.
He was once the president of the of Zionist organization in my hometown.
And I think later on he was secretary.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was the main political endeavor, if you can call it political.
I don't think we were otherwise very politically minded.
My mother-- my mother was actually, she was quite interested in various things.
She was interested in art, She spoke several languages.
And I think she belonged to some associations.
I'm pretty sure she belonged to Vico.
And I think she belonged to a book club where she went, I think once every two weeks or once every month, I don't remember.
But certainly no major political endeavors in my family.
Well, since there were many Germans in my hometown who got pretty vocal, I think in 1938, that's the way I remember it.
It sort of started not being terribly pleasant at the school.
The girls were not as friendly as they used to be, oh yeah, definitely.
But it was nothing overt.
I don't remember having heard-- at that time-- anybody say anything nasty or anything derogatory.
However, in the summer of 1938, actually I was the only one in my school who decided to quit German school.
And I quit on my own.
I wasn't thrown out.
And I decided at that time to transfer to the Czech school.
And in September of '38 I went to the Czech gymnasium.
Actually there, some of the professors were not very pleasant.
Yeah, I forgot to mention, which was interesting, when I decided to quit, our rabbi got together with the director of the school.
And the director of my school was a very decent guy.
He was German but very, very decent and he liked me.
And so they called me in for a conference.
And they-- for half an hour at least, if not longer, they tried really to do to dissuade me from quitting.
Yeah, I'll never forget that.
But I had decided on my own.
And my parents were agreeable to that, to quit and go to Czech school, which I started in September.
At the Czech school, a couple of the professors were not too friendly.
Because, I guess one can understand that-- when somebody had been in the German school for x number of years and all of a sudden because there is danger, he comes running to you.
So they really didn't like the idea.
And there were a couple of other people who had come from the Sudetenland who came to my hometown, after the Sudetenland was already-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no-- how do you say it?
Occupied?
Not occupied but they, they went over to Germany.
I can't remember the word at the moment.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so there were about two or three in my class whose Czech was not the best.
And so our Czech professor was very mean, really.
I remember he flunked me in a dictation because I made two little mistakes.
I've never forgotten him.
And he flunked me, actually, during the grading period.
But then when I left it he gave me-- whatever it was-- the worst grade you can get.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And I sort of started preparing.
I learned cooking.
[LAUGHS] And in April of '39, I was assigned to a place on haksharot in Ivancice.
That's when I joined them.
Haksharot is the agricultural preparation for the life in Palestine.
If you join a settlement, I guess, so you had to know how to work the fields.
And that's what we actually did.
We were a group of about-- I think about 20 people.
There were I believe six or seven girls, and the rest were boys.
We were all between the ages of 17 and 18.
And we worked for a Jewish land owner-- owner.
And they worked us pretty hard, you know, 10 to 12 hours on the field, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [LAUGHS] planting potatoes and planting turnips.
And it was very hard work, because most of us really were not used to physical labor.
I remember coming home at night and-- with my back broken.
But it-- it was-- on the whole, it was a good experience.
And at the beginning it was very hard.
Then it became better.
That was in Ivancice.
I, I was already talking about Ivancice.
That's when I joined, that was in April of '39.
So between January of '39 and April of '39, I was sort of deciding what to do and how to go about it.
And after quitting school, I-- my mother sent me to a cooking school, to learn a little bit.
So I went there for a couple of months.
And I learned cooking and cleaning and all the housework that I never did.
I didn't learn a lot but, a little.
At that time?
Before I joined Ivancice, actually, I was in Prague, because there were no such schools in my hometown.
So my parents sent me to Prague, and I stayed with a family and went to that particular school for two or three months.
I, I think I started in-- in, probably in January.
I don't remember exactly.
I started in January, and I quit, I believe in March.
Because in April I went to that haksharot in Ivancice.
Well, we were told that it's going to be eight to 12 months before we are going to be assigned to a Aliyah Bet transfer.
Because obviously at that time, legal immigration was not possible anymore, or possible only in certain specific cases, which I didn't belong to.
And so we knew we were going with an illegal transport.
And we were told it would take between six and 12 months before we were-- we would be ready.
Well, obviously September the 1st, the war started.
And that changed things drastically.
And we-- we were all sent home and told that they are going to put us on the next transport available.
And during-- so we were sent home sometime, I think the middle of September, I believe.
Because it took a while before everything was arranged.
And the next three months, we were preparing papers, paying taxes, getting passports, being interviewed by the Gestapo, and-- and-- and-- and.
Some people from our group, they were denied passports, out of some technical reasons.
I don't know whether their parents-- they owned something, and they didn't want to give it up or some people had some problems with finances.
I know that about three or four of our group didn't go.
But I had no difficulties.
And after rumors were flying for weeks, changing dates of our departure constantly, finally the departure was set for December the 15th, 1939.
Well, after the right paper, legal immigration to Israel was very difficult.
So the Zionist organizations came up with Aliyah Bet, which was illegal from the point of view of the Palestine authorities but legal from the point of view of the Zionist authorities.
So any Aliyah Bet transport was accepted in Israel-- if they managed to get there-- was accepted by the Zionist authorities as a legal immigration.
And there were a couple of transports, which went before us.
And our transport was supposed to leave illegally, from the German point of view and from the British point of view, but legally from the Zionist point of view.

And so on December the 15th, we-- our transport, which consisted of about 300, I think-- I believe 320 people, went by train to Bratislava, where we were slated to get a boat, taking us down the Danube.
We were supposed to stay in Bratislava only about three days, four days.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were accommodated in a sort of hostel or boarding house, which was called the [NON-ENGLISH], which was originally destined for single people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a pretty poor place.
There was not enough room for us.
We-- there were enough beds, we had to share a bed.
Two girls had to share a bed.
But thinking that we were going to leave in a couple of days, you know, made it all bearable.
Unfortunately, the couple of days dragged on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because of various problems, we stayed in Bratislava for nine months.
And it wasn't very pleasant.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were guarded by the Hlinka Guard.
Originally, we could not leave the place.
Twice a day, we could go to the yard and meet-- the women were somewhere on the first and second floor.
And the men were downstairs and in the basement.
So the only time we could see them was one hour in the morning, on the-- in the yard, at the yard, on the yard, [LAUGHS] and one hour in the afternoon.
And the worst was that nobody knew how long it was going to take.
We were waiting and waiting and waiting and waiting.
And there was always something that did not work.
First there were no boats.
Then the winter came, and the Danube was iced, so we couldn't leave.
Then they wanted to ship us back.
A couple of times they wanted to ship us back because there was not enough money to pay for the accommodations.
And so it-- it wasn't a very pleasant time.
However, I have to add that some of the Bratislava Jews were extremely nice to us and really as helpful as could be.
At that time, they were still living, you know, normally.
There was no problem in Slovakia.
Problems began later.
And I myself was invited by perfect strangers to stay with them.
I stayed with one family for four weeks.
And I stayed with another family for two weeks, and, you know, in relative luxury.
And that was a very pleasant change from the [NON-ENGLISH] In this respect, I was pretty lucky.
Well originally, as I said, the first month or so we could not leave.
And then eventually, yes, they gave us permission.
Originally they gave us permission to leave for a day.
And later on-- I think the people paid for us, when-- you know, they gave them some money, in order to get us out.
I'm not 100% sure of that.
But somehow I have the feeling that they had to pay some money to get us out.
And actually the people I stayed with were really quite rich.
So I'm sure that-- I don't know how much they have to pay.
I'm sure they must have had to pay something.
But I guess they could well afford it.
And they were really super nice.
So we, we got permission.
We got a permission slip.
Anytime we could leave the [NON-ENGLISH], we had to leave with some sort of permission slip.
But they handed them out.
At the-- not at the beginning, but eventually, relatively freely.
Because I remember I went out quite a few times.
We went a couple of times to the movies.
Once we had-- we got some money.
Our parents somehow managed to get some money to us eventually.
I mean it took a while.
At the beginning we didn't have any money.
I remember when the guys took me out the first time that the man of the house pulled out 100 crowns and sort of put it in my pocket, I almost died.
Because that had never happened to me before in my life.
And I was always as a child told you can't accept anything from strangers.
And when he gave me those 100 crowns, it was terrible.
But eventually, I think-- I remember my parents managed to get some money to me, so I had some money.
And originally I remember I had a friend, sort of an admirer, who used to ask me out.
And we used to go to various coffee shops and maybe to movies.
And whenever he asked me out, I always said, you know, I don't have any money.
I can't pay.
He said, don't worry, I'll write it down.
OK.
So that went on for a couple of months, and then I got some money.
And so I went to him, and I said, how much do I owe you, expecting him to say nothing or maybe, I don't know, 10 crowns, or 20 crowns.
However, he pulled out the piece of paper out of his pocket and said-- [LAUGHS]
Oh, OK.
